blocks.content: Unlike Ms. Frizzle, Ms. Bron wasn’t big on seatbelts. If you asked her why, she would say it ruined the momentary zero-G experiences as she floored the gas pedal of our blue-and-white magic school bus, hitting each of the 3 speed bumps leading up to my elementary school.

But what was perhaps more interesting was where we were coming back from. And no, we did not travel back in time to the Cretaceous Period, fly into outer space, or go inside a human body. But we did go to a rock and gem show once, a couple of homeless shelters, and a forest at 9 in the evening.
Why 9 in the evening?
Because Ms. Bron wanted us to march through a forest holding the clay hobbit feet we had made to emulate the trek through Mirkwood that Bilbo Baggins and the dwarves did in The Hobbit.
Reflecting back on these memories, I feel a pang of regret.

The memories themselves were great. But many of them, I admit, were largely forgotten once I entered high school. Only when university applications rolled along did these memories resurface. Told in a stifled and insincere way in hopes of “standing out”.

While I could chalk it up to excuses about grades and competition, deep down, I know it wasn’t those reasons. Yes, like many, I got swept up in the resume-building whirlwind, but I never felt like I went too far. I didn’t lose sleep over bad marks. Didn’t break down from the failures in my extracurriculars as if they were my entire identity.
The problem instead was not pushing hard enough the other way. I was not consumed by the environment, yet I participated. And once I participated, I let myself forget about those memories.
I forgot about the photography unit we did. How happy I felt spending those afternoons with an iPad, taking pictures.
Close-ups of the golden brown leaves that seemingly glowed behind the background of the deep green grass.
Landscapes of the tender blue waves melting into the solid jagged rocks, with a piece of the weathered brown wood of a bridge in the foreground.

I forgot about the documentary on homelessness we made. The days spent travelling to various homeless shelters, listening to the stories of the volunteers and those who lived there. These stories, illustrating pasts of complex memories, instilled in me the importance and intrigue of creating a storyline that best captured these moments. But more importantly, it exposed all of us to the nuances of the world, adding a crucial layer to the perceptions we began forming about society as children.
Ms. Bron showed me how to learn unconventionally. Exposed me to activities I felt so much joy doing. Yet I chose to forget. Chose to move on.
Until recently.

Within days after graduating high school, I found myself in front of a blank Notion page. I wanted to start a project. One that I wanted to do fully for myself. As I began my initial brainstorm, I wrote an idea that I later bolded.
“A quest for happiness.”
It was the broadest thing imaginable, with no specificity at all.
I began thinking of how I could implement the scientific method. What experiments I could do. What quantitative evidence I should collect. How would I quantify happiness? Out of 10? Out of 100?
As much as I want to say it was then Ms. Bron’s voice suddenly popped into my head, telling me to learn unconventionally, the wording was a bit different.
They were more in the form of a realization: This isn’t a school project. This is *my* project. And I could do whatever the hell I wanted. Screw the scientific method.
However, if you look closely, the remnants of the elementary school child who sat in Ms. Bron’s magic school bus were still there.
The bullet point above that said “Photography”, despite having done no photography at all since then.
The sudden desire to start a random project merely days after graduation, throwing all the steps of starting a project I had learned in high school out the window.

The final product of the project, a documentary whose storyline captured the moments that best illustrated complex memories, with the aim of adding a crucial layer to the perceptions about happiness we have formed in society.
I regret forgetting. But at the same time, in order to remember, you must forget.

On the last day of The Hobbit unit, Elizabeth, her students and their parents enjoy a Hobbit banquet in the Student Center. The table is set with ceramic goblets, which the students make themselves, and their own Hobbit foot-shaped candleholders. They enjoy roast chicken, “mead” and apple pie. After dinner Elizabeth, dressed as the character Gandalf, takes the group on a real journey through Tynehead Park in the moonlight.
